cve_desc: Depending on the way the format strings in the card label are crafted it's possible to leak kernel stack memory. There is also the possibility for DoS due to the v4l2loopback kernel module crashing when providing the card label on request (reproduce e.g. with many %s modifiers in a row).

finding.short_description: Format string vulnerability: user-controlled card_label used as snprintf format string
finding.long_description: The dev->card_label field is passed directly as the format string argument to snprintf(). If card_label contains format specifiers (e.g., %s, %n, %x), an attacker who can control the card_label value could read from or write to arbitrary memory locations. This could lead to information disclosure, denial of service (crash), or potentially arbitrary code execution. The correct fix is to use snprintf(cap->card, labellen, "%s", dev->card_label) or strlcpy/strncpy instead.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-2652: a format string vulnerability where dev->card_label is used as the format string in snprintf, fixed by adding "%s" as an explicit format specifier.

actual_issue_summary: Format string vulnerability in snprintf() call where dev->card_label is passed directly as the format string, allowing kernel stack memory leaks and DoS via crafted format specifiers. Fixed by adding explicit "%s" format specifier.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Null pointer dereference when graph.GetNode returns nullptr
finding.long_description: graph.GetNode(tensor_id.node()) can return nullptr if the node name is not found in the graph. The result is immediately dereferenced via *input_node without a null check, leading to undefined behavior (typically a crash). An attacker who can control the graph definition (e.g., by supplying a crafted model) could trigger this by providing an Identity node whose input references a non-existent node name.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes the CVE-2022-23589 vulnerability in IsIdentityConsumingSwitch: graph.GetNode can return nullptr and is dereferenced without a null check. The CWE-476, the code location, and the exploitation scenario all match.

actual_issue_summary: In IsIdentityConsumingSwitch, graph.GetNode() can return nullptr when a node is missing from the graph (e.g., from a maliciously altered SavedModel), and the result is dereferenced without a null check, causing a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a nullptr check before the dereference.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Unchecked iterator dereference on outputs map lookup may cause null pointer dereference
finding.long_description: After finding kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in the signature_def map, the code calls .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on the outputs sub-map but never checks whether the returned iterator equals outputs().end(). If the key is not present in the outputs map, dereferencing the end iterator is undefined behavior, which can lead to a crash or potentially exploitable memory corruption. An attacker who can supply a crafted SavedModel with a signature_def entry that has the expected top-level key but lacks the corresponding output key could trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE: the null pointer dereference in GetInitOp caused by not checking the result of find() on the outputs sub-map, which is precisely what the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: In GetInitOp, after finding kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in the signature_def map, the code dereferences the result of find() on the outputs sub-map without checking if the key exists, leading to a null pointer dereference crash when the key is missing.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Memory leak of QuantileStreamResource when resource already exists
finding.long_description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the code silently ignores the error but never frees the newly allocated `result` object. The `new QuantileStreamResource(...)` is allocated on the heap, and if the resource already exists, CreateResource does not take ownership of it. Since no `delete` or `Unref` is called on `result` in the ALREADY_EXISTS path, the object is leaked. An attacker could repeatedly invoke this op to cause unbounded memory consumption, leading to a denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential memory leak unrelated to the CVE. The CVE is about missing validation of negative num_streams leading to a crash via implicit conversion to a large unsigned value in reserve(). The diff only adds a check for num_streams >= 0.

finding.short_description: Missing validation of num_streams input which could be zero or negative
finding.long_description: The `num_streams` value is read directly from user-controlled input and passed to the QuantileStreamResource constructor without any validation. A zero or negative value could lead to unexpected behavior such as division by zero, zero-size allocations, or integer overflow within the resource constructor or its subsequent usage. An attacker could supply a crafted value to trigger undefined behavior or denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the missing validation of num_streams, which is exactly the CVE. A negative num_streams value gets implicitly converted to a large unsigned value in reserve(), causing a crash. The fix adds exactly this validation (num_streams >= 0).

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation that num_streams is non-negative in boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource. A negative value gets implicitly converted to a large unsigned integer when passed to vector::reserve(), causing a crash (denial of service). The fix adds an OP_REQUIRES check that num_streams >= 0.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via a malformed MP4 file in the svc_parse_slice function in av_parsers.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access when indexing avc->sps with unvalidated sps_id
finding.long_description: The value si->pps->sps_id is read from a previously stored PPS entry and combined with GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT to index into the avc->sps array, but neither sps_id nor the resulting sum is validated against the bounds of the sps array. A crafted bitstream could set a PPS's sps_id to a large value, causing an out-of-bounds read (and subsequent use of the resulting pointer), potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash. An attacker can craft a malicious stream where a valid PPS (with slice_group_count != 0) has an arbitrary sps_id value, which is then used unchecked here.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix adds a check for negative pps_id values (pps_id < 0) to prevent out-of-bounds access on the avc->pps array. The finding describes a different issue — unvalidated sps_id when indexing avc->sps — which is not what this CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a buffer overflow caused by a negative pps_id value (returned by gf_bs_read_ue_log) being used to index the avc->pps array without a lower-bound check. The fix adds a (pps_id < 0) check to prevent out-of-bounds array access.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow when loop variable `int i` is compared against `int64_t nnz`, potentially causing undefined behavior and out-of-bounds access.
finding.long_description: The variable `nnz` is declared as `int64_t` and derived from the first dimension of user-controlled sparse indices. The loop iterates with `int i`, which is a 32-bit signed integer. If `nnz` exceeds `INT_MAX` (2^31 - 1), the comparison `i < nnz` will always be true because `i` will overflow (signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C++). In practice this can lead to an infinite loop or, more critically, `i` wrapping around to negative values and being promoted back to a large positive `int64_t`, causing out-of-bounds reads on `indices_mat` and out-of-bounds writes on `dense_gathered_flat`. An attacker can craft a sparse tensor with a very large number of non-zero entries to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about integer overflow when constructing a TensorShape directly from user-provided dimensions via `TensorShape(shape_vec)`. The fix replaces this with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape()` which validates the dimensions and returns an error instead of triggering CHECK failures or large allocations. Finding 0 describes a different hypothetical issue about int vs int64_t loop variable mismatch, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability fixed.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is an integer overflow when constructing a TensorShape directly from user-provided sp_shape values using `TensorShape(shape_vec)`. Malicious users could provide very large dimension values that cause integer overflow during shape construction, leading to OOM-based denial of service or CHECK-failure crashes. The fix uses `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape()` which validates dimensions and returns an error status instead of crashing.

cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

finding.short_description: Division by zero when bi->pixelsize is 0
finding.long_description: The value of bi->pixelsize is read directly from the input file via dbuf_getu16be and used as a divisor without any validation. A crafted file can set this field to 0, causing a division by zero, which leads to a crash (undefined behavior in C). An attacker can exploit this by providing a malformed PixMap header to cause a denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-28856: division by zero in src/fmtutil.c due to pixelsize being zero, with the correct CWE-369 and the correct code location.

actual_issue_summary: Division by zero in fmtutil_macbitmap_read_pixmap_only_fields when bi->pixelsize (read from input) is 0, used as divisor in the expression (bi->rowbytes*8)/bi->pixelsize. Fixed by adding a check that bi->pixelsize > 0 before performing the division.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow check is bypassed due to implicit truncation from int64_t to int
finding.long_description: The variable `new_num_elements` is declared as `int` (32-bit), but `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` operates on and returns `int64_t` (64-bit) values. When the 64-bit result is assigned to the 32-bit `new_num_elements`, it is silently truncated. A large product that would be detected as overflow in 64-bit space could truncate to a small positive 32-bit value, bypassing the `new_num_elements < 0` overflow check. This could allow an attacker to supply a crafted `sparse_shape` with large dimension values that pass the overflow check but result in incorrect downstream behavior, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access or other memory safety issues when constructing the TensorShape or SparseTensor.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow vulnerability (CWE-190) in AddManySparseToTensorsMap. The faulty overflow check using a 32-bit `int` for `new_num_elements` when `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` returns `int64_t` means the check can be bypassed, and then `TensorShape tensor_input_shape(input_shape_vec)` is called with unchecked user-provided dimensions, causing a CHECK-fail. The fix removes this faulty check and replaces it with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape` which properly validates dimensions.

actual_issue_summary: The overflow check in AddManySparseToTensorsMap used an `int` variable (`new_num_elements`) to store the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` (which returns `int64_t`), causing silent truncation that could bypass the overflow detection. Additionally, `TensorShape` was constructed directly from user input without proper validation. The fix replaces the faulty manual overflow check with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape` which properly validates dimensions and returns an error status on overflow.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the compile_xclass_matchingpath() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This involves a unicode property matching issue in JIT-compiled regular expressions. The issue occurs because the character was not fully read in case-less matching within JIT.

finding.short_description: Impossible condition due to comparing *cc to two different constants simultaneously, leading to incorrect min/max range computation
finding.long_description: The condition `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` is always false (unless PT_CLIST and XCL_PROP happen to have the same numeric value), because `*cc` cannot equal two different constants at the same time. The intended check was almost certainly `*cc == PT_CLIST && cc[-1] == XCL_PROP` (checking the property type and the preceding prop/notprop flag). Because this condition is always false, the min/max character range is never updated for PT_CLIST entries with XCL_PROP. This results in incorrect bounds being passed to `read_char()`, which could cause the JIT-compiled code to generate incorrect character reading sequences. Depending on the `read_char` implementation, wrong min/max bounds could lead to reading fewer bytes than necessary for multi-byte characters (in UTF-8/16 modes), potentially causing out-of-bounds reads or incorrect matching behavior that could be exploited to bypass security-sensitive pattern matching (e.g., input validation filters using PCRE2).

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed by the commit: the condition `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` should have been `*cc == PT_CLIST && cc[-1] == XCL_PROP`. The finding accurately explains that this always-false condition causes incorrect min/max range computation, which leads to the character not being fully read in caseless matching within JIT — exactly matching CVE-2022-1586's description of an out-of-bounds read due to a unicode property matching issue in JIT-compiled regular expressions. While the CWE differs (CWE-670 vs CWE-125), the finding describes the root cause that leads to the OOB read.

actual_issue_summary: Bug in compile_xclass_matchingpath where `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` should be `*cc == PT_CLIST && cc[-1] == XCL_PROP`. The always-false condition prevents proper min/max character range calculation for caseless sets, causing incorrect value reading in JIT that leads to out-of-bounds reads during unicode property matching.

cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

finding.short_description: TOCTOU race: uuid_mutex is released while device pointer is still in use
finding.long_description: The function releases uuid_mutex before calling btrfs_shrink_device() and btrfs_reada_remove_dev(), then re-acquires it. During this window, the device pointer obtained under the lock could become invalid if another thread concurrently modifies the device list (e.g., another rm_device or device replace operation). Additionally, the device's writeable state was already modified (rw_devices decremented, dev_alloc_list removed) before the lock was dropped, meaning a failure in btrfs_shrink_device requires an error_undo path that re-adds the device — but another thread could have observed the intermediate state. This race window could lead to use-after-free or inconsistent filesystem state.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2021-3739 is a NULL pointer dereference caused by calling strcmp() on a NULL device_path. The fix adds a NULL check for device_path before the strcmp call. This finding describes an unrelated TOCTOU race condition involving mutex release, which is not the vulnerability addressed by this CVE.

actual_issue_summary: NULL pointer dereference in btrfs_rm_device when device_path is NULL and strcmp(device_path, "missing") is called without a NULL check. The fix adds a `device_path &&` guard before the strcmp call.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Missing validation for negative permutation indices allows out-of-bounds access
finding.long_description: The code validates that `in_idx >= rank` but does not check for negative values of `in_idx`. Since `in_idx` is an `int64_t` derived from user-controlled tensor data, a negative value would bypass the bounds check and be passed to `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`. Depending on the implementation of `Dim()`, this could result in out-of-bounds memory reads, information disclosure, or a crash. An attacker can craft a permutation tensor with negative values to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the exact vulnerability fixed by the commit: negative perm values are not validated, leading to heap buffer overflow via c->Dim(input, in_idx). The fix adds `in_idx <= -rank` check. While the finding uses CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation) instead of CWE-703, it describes the same root cause and consequence.

actual_issue_summary: The shape inference function for Transpose only checked `in_idx >= rank` but not for negative values in the perm tensor. Negative perm indices could cause a heap buffer overflow when passed to `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`. The fix adds `in_idx <= -rank` to the bounds check.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

finding.short_description: TOCTOU: stale `length` used after observable callbacks may modify the array
finding.long_description: The `length` of the array is read once at the start. Subsequently, `njs_value_to_integer` is called on user-supplied arguments (for `start` and `delete`), which can invoke `valueOf`/`toString` callbacks. These callbacks can modify the underlying array (e.g., shrink it, change its length, or cause reallocation). After these calls, the stale `length` is used to compute `start`, `delete`, and bounds for element access. In the fast-array path, `array->start[n]` is accessed with indices derived from the stale length, potentially reading out-of-bounds memory if the array was shrunk or reallocated during the callback. Similarly, `njs_array_copy_within` and `memcpy` operations in the second fast-array block rely on `array->length` which may no longer be consistent with the stale `length`-derived values, leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about uninitialized elements in the 'deleted' fast array when 'this' is a slow array with gaps. The fix adds `njs_set_invalid(&deleted->start[i])` for missing properties. The finding describes a completely different issue (TOCTOU/stale length after callbacks), which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In Array.prototype.splice(), when 'this' is not a fast array but the 'deleted' result array is a fast array, gaps in 'this' (properties that don't exist) left corresponding elements in 'deleted' uninitialized. Later enumeration of the deleted array (via njs_value_own_enumerate) would encounter these uninitialized values, causing a segmentation fault. The fix initializes those gap entries with njs_set_invalid().

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when indices matrix has zero columns but non-zero rows
finding.long_description: When the `indices` tensor has a shape like [N, 0] (N > 0 rows, 0 columns), it passes the `IsMatrix` check and `NumElements()` returns 0, satisfying the emptiness check (requiring `num_values` also be 0). However, the loop iterates `indices->dim_size(0)` times (N times), and accesses column 0 via `indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0)` on a matrix with no columns. This results in an out-of-bounds memory read. An attacker could craft such inputs to read adjacent memory, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

judgments.reasoning: This describes a specific consequence of the missing validation that the CVE addresses. The fix adds `dense_shape->NumElements() > 0` and `dense_shape->shape().dim_size(0) == indices->shape().dim_size(1)` checks which would prevent this scenario. This is a component of the incomplete precondition validation described by CVE-2022-21736.

finding.short_description: Missing validation that indices rows match values length when both are non-empty
finding.long_description: The code only checks that `num_indices == num_values` when one of them is zero. When both are non-zero, there is no check that `indices->dim_size(0) == values->dim_size(0)`, which is the necessary consistency constraint (indices is [N, rank], values is [N]). Furthermore, `num_indices` is the total element count of the matrix (N*rank), not the number of rows, so even the zero-case comparison is semantically wrong. A mismatch between the number of index rows and the number of values could lead to out-of-bounds accesses in downstream SparseTensor operations if `SparseTensor::Create` does not independently validate this.

judgments.reasoning: This describes another component of the incomplete validation that CVE-2022-21736 addresses. The fix replaces the flawed emptiness check with a proper `values->shape().dim_size(0) == indices->shape().dim_size(0)` check. This missing validation is part of the preconditions that the CVE description says are not validated.

actual_issue_summary: SparseTensorSliceDataset lacked proper validation of the relationship between its three sparse tensor inputs (indices, values, dense_shape). Missing checks included: values count matching indices rows, dense_shape dimensions matching indices columns, and dense_shape having at least one element. These missing preconditions could lead to nullptr dereference and undefined behavior.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Shared member variables accessed outside the mutex lock leading to a data race.
finding.long_description: The mutex `mu_` is released after the initial block, but the code then proceeds to read shared member variables `fname_to_index_` and `sss_[idx]` (as well as using `tss->shape()`) without holding the lock. If another thread concurrently modifies these data structures (e.g., via `LoadAllShards` or other mutations), this constitutes a data race. This could lead to reading inconsistent or freed data, potentially causing crashes, memory corruption, or exploitable undefined behavior.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about missing validation of tensor data size when loading checkpoints, allowing buffer overflow in CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice. The fix adds a size check before that call. This finding describes a data race issue which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation that the tensor proto data size matches the expected number of elements for a tensor slice before calling CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice, allowing a buffer overflow when loading crafted/malicious checkpoints with insufficient data.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Potential division by zero when N is zero (though guarded) or integer division truncation leading to shape mismatch
finding.long_description: While N>0 is checked, if num_updates is not evenly divisible by N, the integer division num_updates/N truncates, causing N*(num_updates/N) != num_updates. This means the shaped<T,2> reshape will operate on fewer elements than updates actually contains, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access or incorrect computation. An attacker could craft indices and updates tensors where num_updates % N != 0 to trigger undefined behavior.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about division by zero in the scatter division operation itself (ResourceScatterDiv divides params by updates values that can be zero). This finding describes a completely different issue about integer division truncation in shape computation, which is unrelated to the CVE.

code_snippets:
const Index bad_i = functor(c, c->template eigen_device<Device>(),
                                    params_flat, update, indices_flat);
finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds indices validation happens after the scatter operation, not before
finding.long_description: The scatter functor executes the scatter operation and returns a bad index only after it has already written to (or read from) out-of-bounds memory in params_flat. If an attacker provides an index outside [0, params->dim_size(0)), the write/read occurs first, and only then is the error reported. This allows out-of-bounds read/write on the params tensor buffer, potentially leading to information disclosure or memory corruption.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about division by zero when updates contain zero values and the operation is ResourceScatterDiv. This finding describes a different potential issue about out-of-bounds index validation ordering, which is unrelated to the CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: The ResourceScatterDiv operation performs element-wise division of resource variable values by update values, but did not validate that update values are non-zero. The fix adds a check that rejects updates containing zero values when the operation is DIV on CPU, preventing a floating-point division by zero error.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to 2.1-DEV.

finding.short_description: Untrusted bitstream value used for large memory allocation without bounds checking
finding.long_description: NbBits is read as a 5-bit value (0–31) and then nbFields is read using NbBits bits, meaning nbFields can be up to 2^31 - 1. This attacker-controlled value is passed directly to gf_sg_vrml_mf_alloc to allocate an array, and is also used as the loop bound for iterating and decoding fields. A crafted bitstream can specify an extremely large nbFields value, causing excessive memory allocation (denial of service) or, if the element size multiplied by nbFields overflows internally, a heap buffer overflow when the loop writes beyond the undersized allocation.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about use-after-free (CWE-416). The fix changes early 'return e' statements to 'goto exit' so that QP cleanup code always runs on error paths, preventing use-after-free of QP nodes. This finding describes a completely different issue (integer overflow / excessive allocation from untrusted input) that is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In the MFNODE decoding branch, early 'return e' on error skipped QP cleanup code (gf_bifs_dec_qp_remove), leaving stale QP node references in the codec. The fix replaces these early returns with 'goto exit' to ensure QP cleanup always executes, preventing use-after-free of QP nodes.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Missing validation of axis_ against input tensor dimensions leads to out-of-bounds access
finding.long_description: The code uses axis_ to index into the input tensor's dimensions via input.dim_size(axis_) without first checking that axis_ is less than input.dims(). If axis_ is greater than or equal to the number of dimensions of the input tensor, this results in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker who can control the axis attribute and the input tensor shape could trigger undefined behavior or a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE explicitly states that validation should check that axis is a value in range for the rank of the input tensor. The fix adds exactly this check (input.dims() > axis_). This finding correctly identifies one component of the CVE.

finding.short_description: No validation that min/max range tensors have num_slices elements, leading to out-of-bounds read
finding.long_description: The loop iterates num_slices times and accesses min_ranges(i) and max_ranges(i), but there is no check that input_min_range and input_max_range tensors actually contain at least num_slices elements. If an attacker provides min/max range tensors with fewer elements than the number of slices along the given axis, the indexed accesses will read out of bounds, potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE explicitly states that validation should check that the lengths of min_range and max_range inputs match the axis dimension of the input tensor. The fix adds checks that min_range and max_range are 1-D tensors with size matching num_slices. This finding correctly identifies another component of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Incomplete validation in tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2: no check that axis is within valid range for the input tensor's rank, no check that min_range and max_range tensor sizes match the axis dimension, and no check that min_range/max_range contain exactly one element when axis is -1. This allows null pointer dereference and out-of-bounds heap access.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

finding.short_description: Signed-to-unsigned integer conversion allows negative index to become a very large value
finding.long_description: The input scalar is read as a signed int but immediately stored into a std::size_t (unsigned). A negative input value will wrap around to a very large unsigned value, which is then passed to buf->Peek(). Depending on the Peek implementation, this could lead to an out-of-bounds read, denial of service, or other undefined behavior. An attacker who controls the input tensor can supply a negative integer to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-29195) is specifically about the lack of validation that the input tensor is a scalar before calling .scalar<int>()(). Passing a non-scalar tensor triggers a CHECK failure (denial of service). The finding describes a different issue — signed-to-unsigned integer conversion — which is not what the CVE is about. While the same line of code is involved, the finding identifies a different vulnerability mechanism than the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The implementation of tf.raw_ops.StagePeek calls .scalar<int>()() on the input tensor without first validating that it is actually a scalar. Passing a non-scalar tensor triggers an internal CHECK failure, resulting in a denial of service. The fix removed the index-based Peek call entirely, replacing it with a Get call.

We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.long_description: The function checks that indices is a matrix and values is a vector, but never validates that indices.dim_size(0) == values.dim_size(0). If a caller provides mismatched sizes, the downstream functor (SparseFillEmptyRows) will likely perform out-of-bounds memory reads or writes when iterating over indices rows and accessing the corresponding value, leading to potential information disclosure or memory corruption.

actual_issue_summary: SparseFillEmptyRows lacks validation that the first dimension of `indices` matches the length of `values`, allowing mismatched sizes to cause heap out-of-bounds reads. The fix adds a check that indices_t.dim_size(0) == values_t.dim_size(0).

cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

finding.short_description: USB device reference leak on error path
finding.long_description: When hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the error path jumps to err_free which only calls kfree(priv) but does not call usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) to release the reference obtained by usb_get_dev(). This leads to a reference count leak on the USB device, preventing it from ever being freed, which constitutes a resource leak (memory/device leak) that could be triggered repeatedly to exhaust kernel memory.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE: a memory/resource leak due to missing usb_put_dev() in the error path when hid_parse() fails. CWE-401 (memory leak) is consistent with the CVE's CWE-200 classification and the actual fix.

actual_issue_summary: Missing usb_put_dev() call in the error handling path of elo_probe(). When hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the code jumps to err_free which frees priv but doesn't release the USB device reference obtained via usb_get_dev(), causing a reference count leak / memory leak.

cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

finding.short_description: Use-after-free: ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen accessed after freeaddrinfo()
finding.long_description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, all addrinfo nodes in the linked list (including the one pointed to by ptr) are freed. The code then dereferences ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen in the getsockname() call and subsequent net_port_get/net_address_get calls. This is a use-after-free vulnerability. Depending on heap allocator behavior, this could lead to reading/writing freed memory, which may cause crashes, information disclosure, or potentially arbitrary code execution if an attacker can influence heap contents between the free and the subsequent use.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-38383: a use-after-free in net_bind() where ptr (from the freed servinfo linked list) is dereferenced after freeaddrinfo(). The CWE-416 matches, and the description accurately identifies the problematic code pattern that the fix addresses by using a local naddr variable instead.

actual_issue_summary: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) frees all addrinfo nodes, the code continues to use ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen in getsockname() and net_port_get/net_address_get calls, constituting a use-after-free. The fix introduces a local union net_sockaddr variable to store the result of getsockname() instead of reusing the freed addrinfo memory.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

finding.short_description: Memory leak of 'a' when dma_get_sgtable fails
finding.long_description: When dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated 'a' structure. This leads to a kernel memory leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated failures of dma_get_sgtable could exhaust kernel memory.

judgments.reasoning: Exactly matches the CVE: missing kfree(a) in the error path when dma_get_sgtable fails, causing a memory leak (CWE-401).

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach(): when dma_get_sgtable() fails, the kzalloc'd 'a' is not freed before returning, fixed by adding kfree(a).

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

finding.short_description: Timing side-channel in password hash comparison using strncmp
finding.long_description: The function uses strncmp to compare password hashes/digests. strncmp is not constant-time: it returns as soon as it finds a differing byte. An attacker can measure the time taken by the comparison to progressively determine the stored hash one byte at a time, eventually recovering the full hash. A constant-time comparison function (e.g., crypto_memneq or a hand-rolled constant-time memcmp) should be used instead.

judgments.reasoning: This directly describes the CVE: strncmp leaks timing information during hash comparison. CWE-208 (Observable Timing Discrepancy) is a more specific child of CWE-200 and accurately characterizes the issue. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq.

finding.short_description: Using strncmp on binary (non-string) data causes incorrect comparison
finding.long_description: strncmp operates on char* and stops at the first null byte ('\0'). Since passwd1_sum and passwd2_sum are cryptographic hashes (arbitrary binary data), they may contain embedded null bytes. If a null byte appears early in the hash, strncmp will consider the remaining bytes equal, drastically reducing the effective comparison length and making it far easier to produce a colliding password. memcmp (or a constant-time equivalent) should be used for binary data comparison.

judgments.reasoning: While using strncmp on binary data is a valid secondary concern, the CVE (CVE-2021-37848) specifically describes timing information leakage. The null-byte truncation issue is a different class of bug (incorrect function usage) and is not what the CVE addresses. The commit message explicitly states the fix is about time-constant comparison.

actual_issue_summary: strncmp is used to compare password hashes in check_passwd(), leaking timing information that could allow an attacker to deduce the stored hash. The fix replaces strncmp with the constant-time crypto_memneq().

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when checking *from after advancing past end
finding.long_description: After incrementing `from` by `step`, the code dereferences `*from` before checking whether `from == end`. Due to C's short-circuit evaluation of `||`, the left operand `data != *from` is evaluated first. If `from` has been advanced to or past `end`, this dereference reads out-of-bounds memory. An attacker providing crafted image data could trigger this to leak information or cause a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue: the condition checks `data != *from` before verifying `from == end`, causing an out-of-bounds access. The fix reorders to `from >= end || data != *from` so the bounds check short-circuits first. While the CVE is classified as CWE-787 (write) and the finding says CWE-125 (read), the underlying bug — wrong order of operands leading to out-of-bounds memory access — is the same vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In pcx_write_rle(), the condition `data != *from || from == end` dereferences `*from` before checking if `from` has reached `end`, causing an out-of-bounds buffer access. The fix reorders to `from >= end || data != *from` so the bounds check short-circuits before the dereference.

cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

finding.short_description: Potential division by zero when computing silence padding size from format width
finding.long_description: The variable `width` is obtained from `snd_pcm_format_physical_width(format)`. If `format` is invalid or unrecognized (e.g., `snd_pcm_oss_format_from` returns an unexpected value), `snd_pcm_format_physical_width` can return 0 or a negative value. Since `width` is declared as `unsigned long`, a negative return value wraps to a large number, but a zero return causes a division-by-zero crash (kernel oops/panic). This is used in two places: `(8 * (...) + 7) / width` and `size * 8 / width`. An attacker who can influence the OSS format parameter (e.g., via ioctl from userspace) could trigger this to cause a denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-3303 is a race condition (CWE-362) where snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() is called before acquiring params_lock, allowing another thread to reconfigure the stream in between and cause a NULL pointer dereference of the OSS buffer. The fix moves the make_ready call inside the mutex. This finding describes a hypothetical division-by-zero issue with the width variable, which is unrelated to the race condition that constitutes the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Race condition in snd_pcm_oss_sync() where snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() was called before acquiring params_lock mutex. Another thread could reconfigure the stream between the make_ready call and the mutex acquisition, leading to inconsistent state and a NULL pointer dereference of the OSS buffer. The fix moves the make_ready call inside the mutex-protected region.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when copying type and class fields without bounds checking against max.
finding.long_description: After advancing the pointer past the DNS name by name_part_len bytes, the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) without verifying that `start + name_part_len + 4 <= max`. A maliciously crafted DNS packet where the name consumes most or all of the remaining buffer would cause reads past the end of the packet buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds memory read. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing bounds check before reading type and class fields in parse_query(), which is exactly what the fix adds (`if (p + 4 > max) return PJLIB_UTIL_EDNSINSIZE`). The CWE differs (CWE-125 vs CWE-787) but the underlying vulnerability is the same - reading/writing beyond buffer bounds due to missing size validation in DNS packet parsing.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflow in PJSIP DNS packet parsing: the parse_query() function (and related get_name_len/get_name functions) lacked proper bounds checking. Specifically, after parsing the DNS name, parse_query() would read 4 bytes for type and class fields without checking that the buffer had enough remaining data, potentially reading/writing beyond the packet buffer. The fix adds a bounds check `if (p + 4 > max)` before these reads.

cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing skb length check before accessing skb->data
finding.long_description: The function dereferences skb->data to read the packet type byte without first verifying that skb->len is at least 1. If the virtio device (or a malicious/buggy backend) delivers a zero-length skb, this results in an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, skb_pull with a length of 1 on a zero-length buffer can lead to an underflow of skb->len (wrapping to a very large value), which could then cause further out-of-bounds access when hci_recv_frame processes the corrupted skb. An attacker controlling the virtio backend could exploit this to leak kernel memory or cause a denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a memory leak (CWE-772) where socket buffers with invalid packet types are never freed due to a missing default case in the switch statement. The finding describes a hypothetical out-of-bounds read from zero-length skb, which is a completely different issue unrelated to the actual CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle(): when a packet with an invalid/unrecognized packet type is received, the sk_buff is never freed because the switch statement lacks a default case to call kfree_skb(skb).

cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

finding.short_description: Off-by-one error allows out-of-bounds access to fixed file table
finding.long_description: The boundary check uses `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` instead of `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`. Since `nr_user_files` represents the count of files (indexed 0 to nr_user_files-1), an `fd` value equal to `nr_user_files` passes the check but results in an out-of-bounds access when used in `io_fixed_file_slot(&ctx->file_table, fd)` and `array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)`. Note that `array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)` when fd == nr_user_files returns 0 (clamped), which mitigates the OOB read in practice, but this still leads to incorrect behavior by operating on the wrong file descriptor (index 0 instead of the intended one), potentially cancelling the wrong I/O operation.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the off-by-one error (CWE-193) in the boundary check `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` that should be `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`, which is precisely the vulnerability fixed by the commit for CVE-2022-3103.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in __io_sync_cancel where the fd boundary check uses `>` instead of `>=` against ctx->nr_user_files, allowing fd equal to nr_user_files to pass validation and cause an out-of-bounds access into the fixed file table.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
